text: Gold(I) Catalyzed Sulfination of Arylboronic Acids Leading to Sulfones and Sulfonamides through Redox-Neutral Addition of SO2 to Arynes Gold catalysis is arguably one of the most popular catalytic reactions discovered in the last two decades. The most prevalent applications include the activation of $\pi$-bonds, particularly alkynes, leading to either formal [2+2] or [2+4] cycloadditions and the corresponding ring formations. Apart from $\pi$-bond activation at one site, there are only a handful of reactions where Au(I) acts at two different sites where some sort of bond activation takes place. In a seminal piece of work, a gold(I)-catalyzed sulfination of aryl boronic acids have been reported for the first time, taking the advantage of the basic organometallic reactivity associated with gold(I)–heteroatom bonds. The sulfination reaction proceeds through an unprecedented redox-neutral cycle involving the addition of SO2 to the in situ formed arynes generated upon the typical gold(I) catalyzed activation/protodelation of the starting aryl boronic acids; the resulting sulfinate can be further converted into sulfones and sulfonamides. Such heterocyclic sulfones and sulfonamides moieties are common pharmacophores in numerous biological active compounds. In this context, a set of Sulphur containing heterocycles have been assembled through diverse gold catalyzed reactions using indazoles as a common core, which allows the in situ click reaction with SO2 and/or sulphonamides, to produce diverse sulfones and sulfonamide derivatives. The efficiency of this strategy, involving the click reaction with SO2 and/or sulphonamide derivatives, has been exemplified by using diverse indazole derivatives, and the resulting diversity is demonstrated via the assembly of several molecular libraries that offer structural diversity for drug discovery and structure-activity relationship studies.
